subject: Physics
q: The energy density associated with electric field E→ and magnetic field B→ of an electromagnetic wave in free space is given by (ε0- permittivity of free space, μ0- permeability of free space)
question: The energy density associated with electric field E→ and magnetic field B→ of an electromagnetic wave in free space is given by (ε0- permittivity of free space, μ0- permeability of free space)
solution:
When an electromagnetic wave propagates from the source, it transfers energy to the objects in its path. Electric and magnetic fields serve as energy reservoirs for electromagnetic waves. An electromagnetic wave contains the same amount of energy as are contained in the electric and magnetic fields together. In such situation, the energy density of the electromagnetic wave is equal to the sum of the energies of the electric and magnetic fields.

The average electric and magnetic energy densities are given by

UE=12ε0E2UB=12B2μ0

Match List I with List II

  List I   List II
A. Gauss’s law of magnetostatics i. ∮E→·da→=1ε0∫ρdV
B. Faraday’s law of electro magnetic induction ii. ∮B→·da→=0
C. Ampere’s law iii. ∮E→·dl→=-ddt∫B→·da→
D. Gauss’s law of electrostatics iv. ∮B→·dl→=μ0I

Choose the correct answer from the options given below:
question:
Match List I with List II

  List I   List II
A. Gauss’s law of magnetostatics i. ∮E→·da→=1ε0∫ρdV
B. Faraday’s law of electro magnetic induction ii. ∮B→·da→=0
C. Ampere’s law iii. ∮E→·dl→=-ddt∫B→·da→
D. Gauss’s law of electrostatics iv. ∮B→·dl→=μ0I

Choose the correct answer from the options given below:
solution:
Gauss’s law of magnetostatics:

∮B→·da→=0

Faraday’s law of electro magnetic induction:

∮E→·dl→=-ddt∫B→·da→

Ampere’s law:

∮B→·dl→=μ0I

Gauss’s law of electrostatics:

∮E→·da→=1ε0∫ρdV

subject: Physics
q: A plane EM wave is propagating along $x$ direction. It has a wavelength of $4 \mathrm{~mm}$. If electric field is in $y$ direction with the maximum magnitude of $60 \mathrm{Vm}^{-1}$, the equation for magnetic field is :
question: A plane EM wave is propagating along $x$ direction. It has a wavelength of $4 \mathrm{~mm}$. If electric field is in $y$ direction with the maximum magnitude of $60 \mathrm{Vm}^{-1}$, the equation for magnetic field is :
solution:
$\mathrm{E}=\mathrm{BC} \Rightarrow 60=\mathrm{B} \times 3 \times 10^8$
$\Rightarrow \mathrm{B}=2 \times 10^{-7}$
Also $\quad \mathrm{C}=\mathrm{f} \lambda$
$\begin{aligned} & \Rightarrow 3 \times 10^8=\mathrm{f} \times 4 \times 10^{-3} \\ & \Rightarrow \mathrm{f}=\frac{3}{4} \times 10^{11} \\ & \Rightarrow \omega=2 \pi \mathrm{f}=\frac{3}{4} \times 2 \pi \times 10^{11} \\ & \Rightarrow \omega=\frac{\pi}{2} \times 10^3 \mathrm{C}\end{aligned}$
$\Rightarrow \quad$ Electric field $\Rightarrow y$ direction
Propagation $\Rightarrow \mathrm{x}$ direction
Magnetic field $\Rightarrow \mathrm{z}$-direction

subject: Physics
q: Select the correct statement from the following :
question: Select the correct statement from the following :
options: Electromagnetic waves cannot travel in vacuum.
Electromagnetic waves are produced by charges moving with uniform velocity.
Electromagnetic waves carry both energy and momentum as they propagate through space.
solution:
Electromagnetic waves do not required any medium to propagate. They can travel in vacuum. They are transverse in nature like light. They carry both energy and momentum.
A changing electric field produces a changing magnetic field and vice-versa. Which gives rise to a transverse wave known as electromagnetic wave.

Match List-I with List II of Electromagnetic waves with corresponding wavelength range:

  List I   List II
(A) Microwave (I) 400 nm to 1 nm
(B) Ultraviolet (II) 1 nm to 10–3 nm
(C) X-Ray (III) 1 mm to 700 nm
(D) Infra-red (IV) 0.1 m to 1 mm

Choose the correct answer from the options given below:
question:
Match List-I with List II of Electromagnetic waves with corresponding wavelength range:

  List I   List II
(A) Microwave (I) 400 nm to 1 nm
(B) Ultraviolet (II) 1 nm to 10–3 nm
(C) X-Ray (III) 1 mm to 700 nm
(D) Infra-red (IV) 0.1 m to 1 mm

Choose the correct answer from the options given below:
solution:
The electromagnetic spectrum is the range of all types of EM radiation. Radiation is energy that travels and spreads out with different energies. In the daily life, two commonly known EM waves are the light wave and the radio wave.

The other types of EM radiation that make up the electromagnetic spectrum are microwaves, infrared light, ultraviolet light, X-rays and gamma-rays.

The wavelengths of different parts of the electromagnetic spectrum are related by the following relation:

λX<λUV<λIR<λMW

subject: Physics
q: The electric field component of a monochromatic radiation is given by <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mover accent="true"><mi>E</mi><mo>→</mo></mover><mo>=</mo><mn>2</mn><msub><mi>E</mi><mn>0</mn></msub><mi>cos</mi><mi>k</mi><mi>z</mi><mi>cos</mi><mi>ω</mi><mi>t</mi><mo> </mo><mover accent="true"><mi mathvariant="normal">i</mi><mo>^</mo></mover></math>, Its magnetic field <math><mover accent="true"><mrow><mi>B</mi></mrow><mo>→</mo></mover></math> is then given by:
question: The electric field component of a monochromatic radiation is given by <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mover accent="true"><mi>E</mi><mo>→</mo></mover><mo>=</mo><mn>2</mn><msub><mi>E</mi><mn>0</mn></msub><mi>cos</mi><mi>k</mi><mi>z</mi><mi>cos</mi><mi>ω</mi><mi>t</mi><mo> </mo><mover accent="true"><mi mathvariant="normal">i</mi><mo>^</mo></mover></math>, Its magnetic field <math><mover accent="true"><mrow><mi>B</mi></mrow><mo>→</mo></mover></math> is then given by:
solution:
dEdz=-dBdt

dEdz=-2E0Ksin⁡kzcos⁡ωt=-dBdt

dB=+2E0Ksin⁡kzcos⁡ωt dt

B= +2E0Ksin⁡kz∫cos⁡ωt dt

=+2E0kωsin⁡kzsin⁡ωt

E0B0=ωk=C

B=2E0Csin⁡kzsin⁡ωt

Propagation of EM radiation, Electric field oscillation and Magnetic field oscillation are perpendicular to each other.

Since propagation of EM radiation is x-axis and electric field oscillation is along z-axis, the Magnetic field oscillation is along y-axis.

subject: Physics
q: The frequency of $X$-rays; $\gamma$-rays and ultraviolet rays are respectively $a, b$ and $c$ then
question: The frequency of $X$-rays; $\gamma$-rays and ultraviolet rays are respectively $a, b$ and $c$ then
solution:
Frequency range of $\gamma$-ray, $b=10^{18}-10^{23} \mathrm{~Hz}$
Frequency range of X-ray, $a=10^{16}-10^{20} \mathrm{~Hz}$
Frequency range of ultraviolet ray, $c=10^{15}-10^{17} \mathrm{~Hz}$
$\therefore a < b ; b>c$

subject: Physics
q:
Match the List-I with List-II:

  List I   List II
A Microwaves I Radioactive decay of the nucleus
B Gamma rays II Rapid acceleration and deceleration of electron in aerials
C Radio waves III Inner shell electrons
D X-rays IV Klystron valve

Choose the correct answer from the options given below:
question:
Match the List-I with List-II:

  List I   List II
A Microwaves I Radioactive decay of the nucleus
B Gamma rays II Rapid acceleration and deceleration of electron in aerials
C Radio waves III Inner shell electrons
D X-rays IV Klystron valve

Choose the correct answer from the options given below:
solution:
(A) A Klystron valve or the magnetron valve are generally used for the production of electromagnetic waves and that electromagnetic wave is Microwave. So, A→IV.

(B) Gamma rays are produced during the disintegration of radioactive atomic nuclei and the decay of certain subatomic particles.So, B→I

(C) Radio waves can be produced by rapid acceleration and deceleration of electrons in aerials (conductors). These can be detected by receivers of aerials. So, C→II

(D) X-rays are produced when a metal target or any other element is bombarded with high energy particles such as electrons, photons or ions. When high energy electrons is incident on a bound electron in an atom, then bound electron emits from the inner shell of that atom. So, D→III

question: The rms value of the electric field of the light coming from the Sun is $720 \mathrm{~N} / \mathrm{C}$. The average total energy density of the electromagnetic wave is
solution:
$U_{a v}=\varepsilon_0 E_{\text {rs }}^2=4.58 \times 10^{-6} \mathrm{~J} / \mathrm{m}^3$
$E_{\text {rms }}=4.58 \times 10^{-6} \mathrm{~J} / \mathrm{m}^3$

subject: Physics
q:
Match the LIST-I with LIST-II
\(\begin{array}{|l|l|l|l|} \hline & \text{List-I} & & \text{List-II} \\ \hline \text{A.} & \text{Radio-wave} & \text{I.} & \text{is produced by Magnetron valve} \\ \hline \text{B.} & \text{Micro-wave} & \text{II.} & \text{due to change in the vibrational modes of atoms} \\ \hline \text{C.} & \text{Infrared-wave} & \text{III.} & \text{due to inner shell electrons moving from higher to lower energy level} \\ \hline \text{D.} & \text{X-ray} & \text{IV.} & \text{due to rapid acceleration of electrons} \\ \hline \end{array}\)
Choose the correct answer from the options given below:
question:
Match the LIST-I with LIST-II
\(\begin{array}{|l|l|l|l|} \hline & \text{List-I} & & \text{List-II} \\ \hline \text{A.} & \text{Radio-wave} & \text{I.} & \text{is produced by Magnetron valve} \\ \hline \text{B.} & \text{Micro-wave} & \text{II.} & \text{due to change in the vibrational modes of atoms} \\ \hline \text{C.} & \text{Infrared-wave} & \text{III.} & \text{due to inner shell electrons moving from higher to lower energy level} \\ \hline \text{D.} & \text{X-ray} & \text{IV.} & \text{due to rapid acceleration of electrons} \\ \hline \end{array}\)
Choose the correct answer from the options given below:
solution:
Matching electromagnetic wave sources:
Radio-wave (A) - produced by rapid acceleration of electrons in antennas and transmitters, matches IV.
Micro-wave (B) - produced by Magnetron valve, matches I.
Infrared-wave (C) - due to change in vibrational modes of atoms and molecules, matches II.
X-ray (D) - due to inner shell electrons moving from higher to lower energy levels, matches III.
Correct pairing: A-IV, B-I, C-II, D-III

solution:

The ratio of the force acting on charge <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>q</mi><mn>1</mn></msub></math> to <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>q</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math> is :
question: The magnetic field vector of an electromagnetic wave is given by <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>B</mi><mo>=</mo><msub><mi>B</mi><mn>0</mn></msub><mfrac><mrow><mover><mi mathvariant="normal">i</mi><mo>^</mo></mover><mo>+</mo><mover><mi mathvariant="normal">j</mi><mo>^</mo></mover></mrow><msqrt><mn>2</mn></msqrt></mfrac><mi>cos</mi><mfenced><mrow><mi>k</mi><mi>z</mi><mo>-</mo><mi>ω</mi><mi>t</mi></mrow></mfenced></math> where <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mover><mi mathvariant="normal">i</mi><mo>^</mo></mover><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mover><mi mathvariant="normal">j</mi><mo>^</mo></mover></math> represents unit vector along <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>y</mi></math>-axis respectively. At <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>t</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mo>,</mo></math> two electric charges <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>q</mi><mn>1</mn></msub></math> of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>4</mn><mi>π</mi></math> coulomb and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>q</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math> of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mi>π</mi></math> coulomb located at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mrow><mn>0</mn><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mn>0</mn><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mfrac><mi>π</mi><mi>k</mi></mfrac></mrow></mfenced></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mrow><mn>0</mn><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mn>0</mn><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mfrac><mrow><mn>3</mn><mi>π</mi></mrow><mi>k</mi></mfrac></mrow></mfenced><mo>,</mo></math> respectively, have the same velocity of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>5</mn><mi mathvariant="normal">c</mi><mover><mi mathvariant="normal">i</mi><mo>^</mo></mover><mo>,</mo></math> (where <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>c</mi></math> is the velocity of light ). The ratio of the force acting on charge <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>q</mi><mn>1</mn></msub></math> to <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>q</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math> is :
solution:
At t=0
B at 0, 0, πk=B0i^+j^2cosπ
B at 0, 0, 3πk=B0i^+j^2cos3π
Force on charged particle q1
F1=q1V→1×B→1
=4π0.5ci^×-B0i^+j^2=4πB0c22-k^
Force on charged particle q2
F2=q2V→2×B→2
=2π0.5ci^×-B0i^+j^2=2πB0c22-k^⇒F1F2=2

B→=B0i^coskz-ωt+B1j^coskz+ωt
B0=3×10-5 T &  B1=2×10-6 T
Electric filed associated with it is,

At z=0,
Frms=QB0c2+QB1c22

=10-4×3×10-5×3×1082+10-4×2×10-6×3×10822
=0.81+0.00362=0.6 N

subject: Physics
q:
The electric field of an electromagnetic wave in free space is $\overrightarrow{\mathrm{E}}=57 \cos \left[7.5 \times 10^6 \mathrm{t}-5 \times 10^{-3}(3 x+4 y)\right](4 \hat{i}-3 \hat{j}) N / C$.
The associated magnetic field in Tesla is
question:
The electric field of an electromagnetic wave in free space is $\overrightarrow{\mathrm{E}}=57 \cos \left[7.5 \times 10^6 \mathrm{t}-5 \times 10^{-3}(3 x+4 y)\right](4 \hat{i}-3 \hat{j}) N / C$.
The associated magnetic field in Tesla is
solution: $\begin{aligned} & \overrightarrow{\mathrm{K}}=3 \hat{\mathrm{i}}+4 \hat{\mathrm{j}} \\ & \hat{\mathrm{K}}=\frac{3 \hat{\mathrm{i}}+4 \hat{\mathrm{j}}}{5} \\ & \hat{\mathrm{E}}=\frac{4 \hat{\mathrm{i}}-3 \hat{\mathrm{j}}}{5} \\ & \hat{\mathrm{~B}}=\hat{\mathrm{K}} \times \hat{\mathrm{E}} \\ & \hat{\mathrm{B}}=-\hat{\mathrm{Z}} \\ & \mathrm{B}_0=\frac{\mathrm{E}_0}{\mathrm{C}}=\frac{57}{3 \times 10^8}\end{aligned}$

question: An electromagnetic wave is represented by the electric filed <math><mover accent="true"><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">E</mi></mrow><mo>→</mo></mover><mo>=</mo><msub><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">E</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>0</mn></mrow></msub><mover accent="true"><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">n</mi></mrow><mo>^</mo></mover><mrow><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">sin</mi></mrow><mo>⁡</mo><mrow><mfenced close="]" open="[" separators="|"><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">ω</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">t</mi><mo>+</mo><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mn>6</mn><mi>y</mi><mo>-</mo><mn>8</mn><mi>z</mi></mrow></mfenced></mrow></mfenced><mo>.</mo></mrow></mrow></math> Taking unit vectors in <math><mi>x</mi><mo>,</mo><mi>y</mi></math> and <math><mi>z</mi></math> directions to be <math><mover accent="true"><mrow><mi>i</mi></mrow><mo>^</mo></mover><mo>,</mo><mover accent="true"><mrow><mi>j</mi></mrow><mo>^</mo></mover><mo>,</mo><mover accent="true"><mrow><mi>k</mi></mrow><mo>^</mo></mover><mo>,</mo><mi> </mi></math> the directions of propogations <math><mover accent="true"><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></mrow><mo>^</mo></mover><mo>,</mo></math> is:
solution:
The standard equation of electromagnetic wave E→=E0n^sinωt-kx^

here x^ represents the direction of propagation wave.

On comparing the given equation with the standard equation, the direction of propagation is along line -6y+8z . Thus, unit vector along the direction of propagation is,
s^=-6j^+8k^10=-35j^+45k^

q:
A plane electromagnetic wave propagating in x-direction is described by
Ey=200 V m-1sin1.5×107t-0.05x; The intensity of the wave is :

question:
A plane electromagnetic wave propagating in x-direction is described by
Ey=200 V m-1sin1.5×107t-0.05x; The intensity of the wave is :
(Use ϵ0=8.85×10-12 C2 N-1 m-2)
solution:
The magnitude of the electric field component of the electromagnetic wave is given by

E0=200 V m-1

The formula to calculate the intensity of the electromagnetic wave is given by

I=12ε0cE02   ...1

From equation (1), it follows that

I=12×8.85×10-12×3×108×2002 W m-2=53.1 W m-2

subject: Physics
q: An electromagnetic wave in vacuum has the electric and magnetic fields $\vec{E}$ and $\vec{B}$, which are always perpendicular to each other. The direction of polarization is given by $\vec{X}$ and that of wave propagation by $\overrightarrow{\mathrm{k}}$. Then :
question: An electromagnetic wave in vacuum has the electric and magnetic fields $\vec{E}$ and $\vec{B}$, which are always perpendicular to each other. The direction of polarization is given by $\vec{X}$ and that of wave propagation by $\overrightarrow{\mathrm{k}}$. Then :
solution:
Direction of polarization is parallel to magnetic field,
$\therefore \quad \overrightarrow{\mathrm{X}} \| \overrightarrow{\mathrm{B}}$
and direction of wave propagation is parallel to $\overrightarrow{\mathrm{E}} \times \overrightarrow{\mathrm{B}}$
$\therefore \quad \overrightarrow{\mathrm{K}} \| \overrightarrow{\mathrm{E}} \times \overrightarrow{\mathrm{B}}$

E=BC=6. (Direction of the wave) ‖(E→×B→)⇒i^=j^×k^

q: Two sources of light emit X-rays of wavelength 1 nm and visible light of wavelength 500 nm, respectively. Both the sources emit light of the same power 200 W. The ratio of the number density of photons of X-rays to the number density of photons of the visible light of the given wavelengths is:
question: Two sources of light emit X-rays of wavelength 1 nm and visible light of wavelength 500 nm, respectively. Both the sources emit light of the same power 200 W. The ratio of the number density of photons of X-rays to the number density of photons of the visible light of the given wavelengths is:
solution:
Ps-Power of source

Ps=nhcλ ; n =  no. of photons emitted/s

⇒  n∝λ  ⇒  n2n1=1/500

hcλ=mc2

q:
The unit of $\sqrt{\frac{2 \mathrm{I}}{\epsilon_0 c}}$ is :
(I = intensity of an electromagnetic wave, $\mathrm{c}:$ speed of light)
question:
The unit of $\sqrt{\frac{2 \mathrm{I}}{\epsilon_0 c}}$ is :
(I = intensity of an electromagnetic wave, $\mathrm{c}:$ speed of light)
solution:
$\begin{aligned}
& \mathrm{I}=\frac{1}{2} \epsilon_0 \mathrm{E}_0^2 \times \mathrm{C} \\ & \mathrm{E}_0=\sqrt{\frac{2 \mathrm{I}}{\epsilon_0 \mathrm{C}}}
\end{aligned}$
$E_0$ : electric field N/C

q: The waves emitted when a metal target is bombarded with high energy electrons are
question: The waves emitted when a metal target is bombarded with high energy electrons are
solution: The bombarding electrons can eject electrons from the inner shells of the atoms of the metal target. Those vacancies will be quickly filled by electrons dropping from higher levels, emitting x-rays with sharply defined frequencies associated with the difference between the atomic energy levels of the target atoms. Hence, X-rays are emitted when a metal target is bombarded with high energy electrons.

q:
Magnetic field in a plane electromagnetic wave is given by,

B→=B0sinkx+ωtj^ T. Expression for corresponding electric field will be:

(Where c is speed of light)
question:
Magnetic field in a plane electromagnetic wave is given by,

B→=B0sinkx+ωtj^ T. Expression for corresponding electric field will be:

(Where c is speed of light)
solution:
For electromagnetic wave speed of light is given by

c=E0B0
Electric field is expressed as E0=cB0 , E→ is ⊥ to direction x and direction of B→

The expression of electric field is E→=cB0 sin⁡(kx+wt)k^  V m-1

subject: Physics
q: A plane electromagnetic wave of frequency <math><mn>25</mn><mi></mi><mi mathvariant="normal">G</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">H</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">z</mi></math> is propagating in vacuum along the z-direction. At a particular point in space and time, the magnetic filed is given by <math><mover accent="true"><mrow><mi>B</mi></mrow><mo>→</mo></mover><mo>=</mo><mn>5</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mrow><mn>10</mn></mrow><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>8</mn></mrow></msup><mtext> </mtext><mover accent="true"><mrow><mi>j</mi></mrow><mo>^</mo></mover><mtext> </mtext><mi>T</mi><mo>.</mo></math> The corresponding electric field <math><mover accent="true"><mrow><mi>E</mi></mrow><mo>→</mo></mover></math> is (speed of light <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>=</mo><mn>3</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mn>8</mn></msup><mtext> </mtext><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> )
question: A plane electromagnetic wave of frequency <math><mn>25</mn><mi></mi><mi mathvariant="normal">G</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">H</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">z</mi></math> is propagating in vacuum along the z-direction. At a particular point in space and time, the magnetic filed is given by <math><mover accent="true"><mrow><mi>B</mi></mrow><mo>→</mo></mover><mo>=</mo><mn>5</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mrow><mn>10</mn></mrow><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>8</mn></mrow></msup><mtext> </mtext><mover accent="true"><mrow><mi>j</mi></mrow><mo>^</mo></mover><mtext> </mtext><mi>T</mi><mo>.</mo></math> The corresponding electric field <math><mover accent="true"><mrow><mi>E</mi></mrow><mo>→</mo></mover></math> is (speed of light <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>=</mo><mn>3</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mn>8</mn></msup><mtext> </mtext><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> )
solution:
Given, the electromagnetic wave is propagating in z i.e., +k^ direction.

The relation between the magnitudes of electric field, magnetic field and the speed of light is given by

EB=C

⇒ E=B×C
⇒ E=5×10-8×3×108=15 V m-1

We know that the direction of propagation of electromagnetic wave is along E→×B→.

So, E→=15 i^ V m-1

subject: Physics
q: The amplitude of magnetic field in an electromagnetic wave propagating along <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>y</mi></math>-axis is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>6</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>0</mn><mo> </mo><mo>×</mo><mo> </mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>–</mo><mn>7</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">T</mi></math>. The maximum value of electric field in the electromagnetic wave is
question: The amplitude of magnetic field in an electromagnetic wave propagating along <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>y</mi></math>-axis is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>6</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>0</mn><mo> </mo><mo>×</mo><mo> </mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>–</mo><mn>7</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">T</mi></math>. The maximum value of electric field in the electromagnetic wave is
solution:
The relation between the electric field and magnetic field is given by

E0 = cB0 .

The given data is 

B0=6×10-7 T

c=3×108 m s-1

Thus, the maximum value of the electric field is

E0= 3×108×6×10–7=180 V m-1.

subject: Physics
q:
An electromagnetic wave of frequency 100 MHz propagates through a medium of conductivity, $\sigma=10 \mathrm{mho} / \mathrm{m}$. The ratio of maximum conduction current density to maximum displacement current density is $\_\_\_\_$.
$\left[\text{Take }\frac{1}{4 \pi \epsilon_{\mathrm{o}}}=9 \times 10^{9} \mathrm{Nm}^{2} / \mathrm{C}^{2}\right]$
question:
An electromagnetic wave of frequency 100 MHz propagates through a medium of conductivity, $\sigma=10 \mathrm{mho} / \mathrm{m}$. The ratio of maximum conduction current density to maximum displacement current density is $\_\_\_\_$.
$\left[\text{Take }\frac{1}{4 \pi \epsilon_{\mathrm{o}}}=9 \times 10^{9} \mathrm{Nm}^{2} / \mathrm{C}^{2}\right]$
solution:
For an electromagnetic wave in a conducting medium, the maximum conduction current density is $J_{c,max} = \sigma E_0$ and maximum displacement current density is $J_{d,max} = \epsilon_0 \omega E_0$.
The ratio is: $\frac{J_{c,max}}{J_{d,max}} = \frac{\sigma}{\epsilon_0 \omega}$.
With $\sigma = 10$ mho/m, $f = 100$ MHz
So $\omega = 2\pi \times 10^8$ rad/s, and $\epsilon_0 = \frac{10^{-9}}{36\pi}$ F/m: $\frac{J_{c,max}}{J_{d,max}} = \frac{10 \times 36\pi}{10^{-9} \times 2\pi \times 10^8} = \frac{10 \times 36}{2 \times 10^{-1}} = 1800$.

subject: Physics
q: If frequency of electromagnetic wave is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>60</mn><mo> </mo><mi>MHz</mi></math> and it travels in air along <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">z</mi></math> direction then the corresponding electric and magnetic field vectors will be mutually perpendicular to each other and the wavelength of the wave <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mrow><mi>in</mi><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></mrow></mfenced></math> is :
question: If frequency of electromagnetic wave is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>60</mn><mo> </mo><mi>MHz</mi></math> and it travels in air along <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">z</mi></math> direction then the corresponding electric and magnetic field vectors will be mutually perpendicular to each other and the wavelength of the wave <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mrow><mi>in</mi><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></mrow></mfenced></math> is :
solution:
As speed of electromagnetic wave is given by, c=fλ. Therefore, 

λ=cf=3×10860×106=5 m.

q: The magnetic field in a travelling electromagnetic wave has a peak value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>20</mn><mo> </mo><mo>nT</mo></math>. The peak value of electric field strength is :
question: The magnetic field in a travelling electromagnetic wave has a peak value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>20</mn><mo> </mo><mo>nT</mo></math>. The peak value of electric field strength is :
solution:
E = B c=20×10-9×3×108

=  6 V/m
